text:
Indian Bank: Q4 FY26 & FY26 Concall — Investor-Focused Summary (29 Apr 2026)
1) FY26 scorecard vs priorities: growth delivered, asset quality strong; efficiency still a watch

Management characterized FY26 as “a decent year” with outperformance on “almost all parameters except cost-to-income ratio.”

Balance sheet growth (FY26)

Total business: +12.79%
Deposits: +12.29% (bank remains “very cautious in raising bulk deposits”)
Advances: +13.43%, driven by RAM +15.18%
Retail: +18.72%
MSME: +16.39%
Corporate: +9.19% (vs ~3% last year per management)
Jewel loans: ~+28% to ₹1.27 trillion; management flagged this as a meaningful contributor and a key reason for moderating FY27 advance-growth guidance.

Profitability & margins (Q4 / FY26)

Q4 net profit: ₹3,103 cr (+1.3% QoQ)
FY net profit: ₹12,156 cr (+11.33% YoY)
Q4 operating profit: ₹5,286 cr (+5.21% QoQ); FY op profit +4.83%
Q4 NII: ₹7,109 cr (+3.09% QoQ); FY NII ₹26,915 cr (+6.91%)
NIM: 3.23% (Q4); FY NIM 3.24% (down 17 bps YoY, still within prior guidance)
ROA: 1.28% (Q4); FY ROA 1.31% (down ~1 bp YoY)
ROE: 19.53% (FY)

Operating efficiency

Cost-to-income (FY): 46.03%, but a visible quarterly improvement: 46.90% (Dec) → 44.99% (Mar) (management called out “course correction”).

Treasury & other income

“Other income… marginal decline sequentially… primarily because of the impact on the treasury income.”
Management expects FY27 treasury profit to be lower than FY26 but “impact will not be as severe” because they “have not sold some good yielding securities.”
Indicative FY27 treasury profit expectation: ₹1,000–1,200 cr.
2) Deposit franchise & CASA: stabilization via product + activation + float strategy

CASA

CASA grew +10.85%; CASA ratio improved sequentially after June dip: 38.87% (Sep) → 39.08% → 39.67% (Mar).
FY26 end CASA ratio just below the ~40% aspiration; FY27 guidance: “approximately… around 40%.”

Actions taken to support CASA / float

Re-activated ~34 lakh inoperative accounts → ~₹4,000 cr balances added.
Added “more than 3 lakh salary accounts during the quarter.”
Focus on QR codes/POS to capture operating balances (“we have to rely on float” amid “structural shift… people are becoming investors instead of savers”).
Improved relationship quality: average balances increased materially:
Savings (excluding BSBD): ₹30k → ₹46k
Current accounts: ₹1.88L → ₹2.64L

Bulk deposits

Bulk + CDs together are ~18–19% of deposits; preference to keep “less than 20%.”
Management expects deposit costs remain elevated because “advance growth is outpacing deposit growth.”
3) Asset quality: GNPA drops sharply; SMA improvement; prudence buffer added

Reported asset quality (FY26 end)

GNPA: 3.09% → 1.98% (down 111 bps)
NNPA: 0.19% → 0.15%
PCR: 98.28% (steady)
Slippages: 0.96% (Q4); 0.85% annualized (improved from 1.09%)
Credit cost: 0.47% annualized (Q4); 0.31% (FY)

Recoveries

Q4 recovery: ₹1,499 cr
FY recovery: ₹6,651 cr (in line with guidance; management noted the pool is shrinking, hence lower going forward)

SMA commentary (material operational signal)

SMA ratio trend called out as major improvement: 15.59% (Mar’24) → 18.06% (Mar’25) → 4.73% (Mar’26).
SMA >₹5 cr is only ₹922 cr (management emphasized limited large-ticket stress).
However, management clarified part of SMA-2 reduction is due to classification of “two government accounts” (“no brownie point for that”).
Detailed SMA stock shared later: Total SMA ~₹31,000 cr:
SMA-0 ~₹16,000 cr
SMA-1 ~₹9,000 cr
SMA-2 ~₹6,000 cr

Geopolitical prudence

Despite stating “we are not seeing any stress because of the West Asia crisis,” bank created an additional cushion: ~₹310 cr provision (standard asset buffer).
Another disclosure: “this quarter around ₹500-odd crore we have made in the standard” (standard asset provisioning).
4) FY27 guidance: moderated growth, NIM pressure acknowledged; quality remains central

Management explicitly positioned trade-offs: “we remain very cautious for the bottom line and growth, bottom line and asset quality… growth, we are okay with 1% to 2% less than the industry.”

FY27 guidance

Deposits: 9–11%
Advances: 11–13%
CASA: ~40%
CDR: ~80%
GNPA: 1.50–1.60%
NNPA: <0.25%
NIM: 3.10–3.25% (downward bias; “cost of deposit… will remain elevated”)
ROA: 1.20–1.30%
Credit cost: <1%
Slippage ratio: <1%

Why guidance is conservative vs industry

Jewel loan growth may normalize: “maybe similar kind of growth, we may not see in jewel loan this year,” and jewel loans are ~16–17% of the book (per CEO).

Margins: limited levers

CEO was direct: “very few levers are left” since ~50% of loans are externally benchmark linked.
Some offset via MCLR repricing if deposit costs rise (“part of that will be offsetted by increasing MCLR”).
Strategic tilt to better-yielding mid-corporates: focus on ₹50/₹100 cr to ₹500 cr segment where “we can command little better price.”
5) ECL (Expected Credit Loss) readiness: impact not quantified yet; bank expects to absorb through earnings

Key disclosures were qualitative and timing-focused (final RBI guidelines came “only yesterday”).

CEO: impact “will be a little higher” than draft calculations because HTM book is now included (“earlier it was not part… now HTM book has come”).
Still, management message: “impact is not going to be very huge” given benign asset quality and preparatory buffers.
Absorption timeline improved: earlier stated one year; now: “we will be able to absorb all the impact… six to nine months… between one to three quarters.”
Also clarified spillover possible: “some impact will spill over to the next year also” (i.e., not necessarily fully absorbed within FY27 P&L).
6) Business development, underwriting pipeline, and sectoral appetite

Sanctions and pipeline

FY26 total sanctions: ₹4.26 trillion (retail/agri/MSME/corporate), +62% YoY
Corporate sanctions: ~₹1.31 trillion (~62% growth)
MSME sanctions: +81%; also stated: ~6 lakh loans and ₹48,000 cr to MSME.
Pipeline: ₹51,000 cr, of which ₹34,000–35,000 cr is sanctioned & unavailed (term loans).

Sector focus (FY27 opportunities)

“Green” themes: battery/EV, solar power, plus transmission lines (expects PPP traction)
Data centers: “we are seeing lot of demand”
Road sector: was slower last year; expects improvement this year
MSME stress watch: textiles described as “struggling since last… one year,” with some borrowers not drawing WC due to weak sales; limited leather exposure.

Overseas book growth

Only meaningful overseas presence: GIFT City and Singapore (Sri Lanka “hardly anything”).
Growth driven by syndicated loans and trade finance, not sector-concentrated.
7) Digital & technology: rapid scaling, AI deployment, fraud controls; budget intensity rising

Executive Director (Digital) reported:

Digital business grew 63% to ₹2.72 trillion.
Mobile banking customers: 2.36 crore
Mobile banking transactions: +15%, ~65.8 lakh per month
Digital adoption rate in retail & agri sanctions: 97% (“sanctioned… through digital channel”)

Platforms & partnerships

Implementing “10-plus AI and agentic AI-related platforms” with “more than 160 fintech partners.”
Rolled out CRM platform for retail; to be extended to corporate, including service requests and sales management.
Corporate credit digitization journey “almost complete.”
Fraud & risk: introduced EFRM (“real-time AI-driven behavioral analysis”) to curb “digital frauds… and money mule accounts.”
Cash management: IND Optima, 150+ corporate customers onboarded.
Pipeline items: corporate ecosystem engine, RFP creator, invoice processing automation, lead nurturing, intelligent document reader for legal checks, automation of suspicious transaction reporting, and contact center/grievance automation to reduce TAT.

Digital ROI lens (management candidness)

CEO acknowledged heavy spend with benefits still emerging: “we are spending huge in IT, but benefit… to the extent we desire, is yet to come.”
Still highlighted large indirect savings: ~94% transactions through digital, and branch-served transactions cost “at least 5x higher.”

Digital budget

Indicated to be roughly comparable to employee cost: “budget will be more or less equivalent to the human cost.”
8) Liquidity stance: LCR carried with cushion; not optimized purely for NIM
Average LCR: ~127%
Current LCR: ~123–124%
New LCR guidelines from Apr 1 provide “benefit of 4 to 5 bps” (as stated).
CEO prefers buffer due to market confidence considerations (referenced another bank needing to raise costly liquidity) and noted LCR strength is not from special SLR buying: “we are not making a special effort… Whatever… excess in the SLR book… contributing.”
CEO’s view of “fair” LCR: “115% to 120%.”
9) Other notable items / disclosures
IBPC usage: sold ~₹6,000 cr in Q4; ~₹23,000 cr sold during FY26 (growth moderation lever and risk management posture).
Branch expansion: 102 branches opened; on track versus plan of ~300 branches in three years.
Retail unsecured exposure: personal loans only ~₹6,500 cr; MFI ~₹3,300 cr.
NBFC exposure: reduced by ~₹8,000 cr; ~99% of remaining exposure in AAA/AA/A.
Key actionable investor takeaways
Core franchise momentum is intact (double-digit deposit/loan growth, RAM-led, improving corporate growth), but FY27 guidance is intentionally conservative due to jewel-loan normalization, deposit-cost pressure, and expected weaker treasury.
Margin outlook is the key near-term headwind: CEO explicitly guided lower NIM and admitted “very few levers,” prioritizing CASA stability and mid-corp pricing pockets.
Asset quality trajectory remains a standout strength (GNPA <2%, PCR ~98%, SMA stock and >₹5cr SMA low), and management repeatedly anchored strategy around protecting this.
ECL is the biggest upcoming accounting/earnings swing item, but management is signaling manageable impact and earnings absorption within a few quarters; quantification pending due to fresh final guidelines and HTM inclusion.
Technology spending and AI rollout are accelerating, with clear enterprise initiatives (CRM, EFRM fraud stack, corporate ecosystem tools) and a high digital adoption funnel (97% retail/agri sanctions).
